title: Bramboráky
subtitle: Czech potato pancakes with garlic and marjoram
excerpt: Coarsely grated potato pancakes spiked with garlic, marjoram, and black pepper, fried until crisp on the outside and soft within. The Czech potato pancake, simpler than a Swiss rösti, crispier than a Polish placek, and aggressively flavoured.
sourceNotes: Bramboráky are a traditional Czech potato pancake found across Bohemia, Moravia, and Slovakia (where they are called zemiakové placky). They differ from German Kartoffelpuffer or Swiss Rösti in the seasoning: garlic and marjoram are the defining Czech flavours, and the quantity used is substantial rather than decorative. The pancakes are made from raw, coarsely grated potato mixed with egg, flour, garlic, and dried marjoram, fried in sunflower oil. They are eaten as a main course, a snack, or a street food.

content.text: The most important step in making bramboráky is squeezing the moisture from the grated potato before mixing the batter. Wet potato produces a soggy, steamed pancake that cannot crisp. Squeeze the grated potato in a clean cloth or in batches in your hands, extracting as much liquid as possible before combining with the other ingredients.
Marjoram and garlic are not subtle here. A full teaspoon of dried marjoram and two cloves of garlic per four servings is the Czech proportion. These pancakes should taste of both.

Place the grated {{potato}} in a clean cloth and squeeze firmly to remove as much liquid as possible. Transfer to a large bowl. Add the {{eggs}}, {{plain-flour}}, {{garlic}}, {{marjoram}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper-ground}}. Mix well until the ingredients are evenly combined.

Heat a generous layer of {{sunflower-oil}} in a large frying pan over medium-high heat. Working in batches, drop heaped tablespoons of batter into the hot oil and press gently into rounds about 1 cm thick and 10 cm across. Fry for 3 to 4 minutes per side until deep golden and crisp. Do not move them until the first side is fully set and beginning to colour at the edges. Drain on kitchen paper and keep warm in a low oven. Serve with soured cream.

items.fix: Squeeze the grated potato more aggressively before mixing. The batter should feel fairly dry, if it is wet, add a tablespoon more flour. Ensure the oil is hot before adding the batter
items.cause: Too much moisture in the potato, or oil not hot enough
items.symptom: Pancakes not crisping, steaming and soft

items.fix: Do not turn until the first side is fully set, the edges should look opaque and the surface should no longer look wet. If they still crumble, add another egg to the remaining batter
items.cause: Not enough egg or flour, or turned too early

items.fix: Season assertively. Taste the raw batter and adjust before frying. Potato absorbs salt as it cooks
items.cause: Potato absorbs seasoning heavily, more salt and marjoram than you expect

content.text: Bramboráky are a Czech and Slovak staple, sold at Christmas markets across Central Europe and made at home as a quick, filling main course. They are sold from large flat pans at outdoor events, eaten hot with a paper plate and a spoonful of soured cream, and they are made at home when potato, eggs, and flour are what the kitchen contains. The garlic and marjoram are what make them specifically Czech, German Kartoffelpuffer uses only onion; Swiss rösti uses none of either. The Czech version is the most flavoured of the family.
